content.full_text:
WILLIAMS & WOODS
an association including a professional corporation

Charles J. Williams
a professional corporation
Michael R. Woods
Teresa L. Highsmith
Elizabeth B. Hearey

Muir Parkway
1320 Arnold Drive, Suite 160
Martinez, California 94553

Oct [illegible] Sep 5 [illegible] 510.228.3840
Fax 510.228.1703

September 30, 1992

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Request for Written Advice (Government Code §83114(b))

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This letter requests the Commission to provide written advice with respect to the duties of a member of the Orinda Planning Commission under the Conflict of Interest Provisions of the Political Reform Act.

QUESTION

Can a member of the Orinda Planning Commission participate in governmental decisions regarding real property in which a first deed of trust is held by a bank for which the Planning Commissioner is an employee?

FACTS

Mr. James Roethe is a member of the Orinda Planning Commission. He is employed by the Bank of America as the director of litigation. As an officer of the bank, he receives income in the form of a salary and is also entitled to receive options to acquire stock in the Bank of America at predetermined prices. The Bank of America is a Fortune 500 corporation.

The Orinda Planning Commission is processing an application for the development of a project in the Gateway Valley area of the City of Orinda. The project is a proposal by Pacific New Wave Corporation and is generally described as the "Gateway Valley 90/10 Plan". The project consists of approximately 978 acres and proposes a conference center, golf course facilities, multiple family units, single family lots and permanently protected open space. The project area consists of two parcels, one of 499 acres and one of 479 acres which total 978 acres. One of the parcels comprising the project area of 978 acres is known as the Daly property and consists of 499 acres.

Scott Hallabrin
September 30, 1992
Page 2


The former Security Pacific Bank loaned $6.5 million to Pacific New Wave Corporation secured by a
first deed of trust on the 499 acre parcel (the Daly property). The Security Pacific Bank recently
merged with the Bank of America so that this loan is now held in the loan portfolio of the Bank of
America.

To date, the processing of the Gateway Valley 90/10 Plan has involved a series of public hearings
before the Planning Commission on the EIR and public hearings on various aspects of the Plan itself.
Mr. Roethe participated in these hearings because he was not then employed with the Bank of America.

Future proceedings in which the Planning Commission will be involved with the decision-making
process on the project will include a request to amend the Orinda General Plan in order to conform
with the project, the preparation and recommendation for adoption of a specific plan for the Gateway
Valley, the rezoning of property in the Gateway Valley to permit development of a project as finally
approved, the subdivision of the property into separate parcels and the processing of a development
agreement. The Planning Commission will be a participant in each of these steps.

GENERAL COMMENTS

I have had the benefit of reviewing your responses to the following requests for advice: your file
numbers 1-88-147, 1-89-466, 1-89-537 and 1-91-071. These involve in one way or another whether a
public official can participate in a governmental decision regarding real property on which a bank holds
a security in property or has some indirect connection with a developer and there is some connection
between the public official and the bank.

Since the Bank could be affected indirectly by a decision of the Orinda Planning Commission, it appears
that the appropriate standard for determining whether or not a decision will materially affect the Bank
is determined under regulation 18702.2.

The following information is submitted on the issue of whether there would be any foreseeable financial
effect on the Bank of America irrespective of whether or not the Commission acts favorably or
unfavorably on the project, having in mind the threshold for disqualification.1/

First, I enclose portions of an appraisal of the entire 978 acre parcel dated November 1991 prepared
by Roland H. Burchard. The appraisal puts a fair market value as of November 1991 of $26,900,000
on the entire 978 acre site. However, the $6.5 million loan which the Bank holds is secured by the

1/ I understand the threshold amounts under Regulation 18702.2(a) to be: will the
Commission's decision (a) increase or decrease the Bank's gross revenues by $1,000,000 or
more or (b) result in the Bank incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses of $250,000 or more.

Scott Hallabrin
September 30, 1992
Page 3


Daly property only consisting of 499 acres. I call your attention to pp. 34 and 55-56 of the appraisal for a discussion of the Daly property. The sale of this parcel to Pacific New Wave in September 1990 was for a price of $16.8 million. Extrapolating values from the appraisal for the Bank secured parcel appears that the present value of that parcel exceeds the amount of the loan by an amount sufficient to insure that any action by the Commission would not affect the value of the property in a manner which would materially affect the Bank.

Second, I have discussed with representatives of the Bank of America whether a decision affecting the applicant would affect the Bank assuming the existence of certain facts. For example, one assumption is that the value of the property securing the loan equals or exceeds the amount of the loan, and whether or not the Commission acts on an entitlement, the Bank's ability to achieve repayment of the loan would not be affected. The other assumption is if there were a default of the loan and the Bank foreclosed, the expenses incurred by the Bank would not exceed $250,000. Civil Code section 2924d(b) imposes a cap on foreclosure costs of 1% of the loan amount ($65,000). While it is possible that litigation attendant to foreclosure and/or bankruptcy of the debtor could ensue, thus increasing the costs to the Bank, these are events which are not reasonably foreseeable. Representatives of the Bank have indicated that if the facts were to be as stated (without reference to costs over and above normal foreclosure costs), the financial effect on the Bank would not in their opinion reach the threshold amounts.

Finally, we do not see that there is a disqualifying nexus as described in regulation section 18702.1(d) because Mr. Roethe does not receive, nor will he receive, income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided or hindered by a decision of the Planning Commission.

Thank you for your consideration.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Charles J. Williams
City Attorney

CJW:sb

cc:    Steve Yoken, Esq.
       James Roethe, Esq.
       Irwin Kaplan, Planning Director


orin\gen\hallabr

SEP 24 '92 13:43 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.2/12




APPRAISAL
978.10 ACRES WITHIN THE
GATEWAY VALLEY AREA
OWNED BY PWN
ORINDA, CALIFORNIA




PREPARED FOR:
THE TRUST FOR PUBLIC LAND




NOVEMBER, 1991


ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES
Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:44 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.3/12


                    ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES
                         Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants
                              1350 TREAT BOULEVARD, SUITE 280
                         WALNUT CREEK, CALIFORNIA 94596-2150              R E C E I V E D
                         (415) 935-9200 • FAX: (415) 935-3095
                                                                            DEC 20 1991
ROLAND H. BURCHARD, MAI
                                                                       CITY OF ORINDA
                                                                    CITY MANAGER/CLERK
                    December 19, 1991




                    Mr. Michael Joyce
                    The Trust For Public Lands
                    Western Region
                    116 New Montgomery, Third Floor
                    San Francisco, California  94105

                    Dear Mr. Joyce:

                    In accordance with The Trust For Public Land's recent request and
                    authorization, I have inspected and appraised the approximately
                    978.10 acres of property owned by Pacific New Wave (PNW)
                    Corporation, commonly known as the Gateway Valley -- Orinda,
                    California.

                    The accompanying report describes the area, site, highest and
                    best uses, and my method of appraisal.  It contains pertinent
                    data considered in reaching my valuation conclusions.

                    A summary of salient facts, accompanied by my major valuation
                    conclusions, subject to the definition of value, Assumptions and
                    Limiting Conditions, and Certification contained herein, as of
                    October 16, 1991, are shown in Section I of this draft report.

                    Respectfully submitted,

                    ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES

                    [signature]

                    Roland H. Burchard, MAI

SEP 24 '92 13:45 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.4/12


          Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA              34.


          VIII. PROPERTY SALES HISTORY

                    There have been three transfers on lands within the current
               Pacific New Wave ownership land holdings within the last
               five years. The oldest transfer was between the Daly Estate
               and the Gateway Development Group. This sale transacted in
               August of 1988 for $5,250,000. It is my understanding the
               property transacted on an all cash to new loan basis. This
               acquisition was negotiated and closed prior to the Gateway
               Advisory Committee study period, which concluded with the
               Gateway Advisory report in September of 1990. More
               recently, Pacific New Wave Corporation purchased the Daly
               property for a price of $16,866,708. This is a total of
               approximately $33,821 per acre on the 498.7 acres.
               Terms of
               financing were $10,366,000 in cash to a Security Pacific
               Bank loan of $6,500,000 at prime plus one percent due
               November 1, 1992. This acquisition was negotiated in August
               of 1990 and closed in September of 1990.

                    The Zuckerman property sold in December of 1990 and closed
               in January of 1991 for a price of $9,000,000. This amounts
               to $18,773 per acre for the 479.4 acres of land. Terms of
               this transaction were $2,000,000 in cash with a $7,000,000
               promissory note taken back by the Zuckermans. The note was
               secured by a first lien on the property. It bears no
               interest and has a principle payment of $2,000,000 due on
               July 12, 1991 with a balance due of $5,000,000 on December
               12, 1991. The owner's representative has stated that PNW is
               current on the note and that it will be paid off as planned.
               A more thorough discussion of both the Zuckerman sale and
               Daly parcel sale in August of 1990 will be included in the
               property valuation section. These appear to be arms-length
               transactions.




          ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES          Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:45 HENN ETZEL MELLON                           P.5/12

     Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA            55.

          interesting to note, however, on this property is that
     approximately 50 acres are designated by the City of
     Antioch    as    employment-generating    or    commercial
     purposes. This property sold at a substantial premium
     over residential raw acreage because of this commercial
     potential for office, retail and industrial uses. This
     property has level to rolling topography very suitable
     for a mixed use development.   However, it should be
     noted the City has not formally declared what percent
     of land area will remain open space, appropriate
     densities, dedication requirements, etc.   A lengthy
     annexation review process has only recently been
     underway.   A General Plan change and EIR process is
     several years away according to the developer.
          At $61,000 per acre for raw land, this sale
     demonstrates that agricultural zoned land, with future
     residential and commercial potential, has increased in
     value tremendously over the last five years. Overall,
     the price per acre should be adjusted upward to account
     for the inferior Antioch location.  However, no time
     adjustment is warranted because of the fact that prices
     have stabilized, if not declined slightly, for raw land
     since late 1989 in this Eastern County area.   Overall,
     the sale price should be adjusted downward
     substantially in comparison to the subject property to
     account for this property's higher residential density
     potential, smaller size, lower degree of risk potential
     from the political entitlement process (in Antioch -- a
     more pro-development community), and to account for its
     substantially rolling, easier to develop topography.
     Thus, overall a substantial downward adjustment is
     warranted in comparison to the subject.


          Comparable No. 10A represents the August, 1990 sale of
     the Daly property which is a portion of the subject
     Pacific New Wave holdings.   This 498.7-acre property
     sold for $16,866,708.  The purchase price was cash to a
     new Security Pacific loan.  The price per acre equates
     to $33,821.   It is interesting to note that this
     property sold for $5,215,000 from the Daly Estate in
     August of 1988.  The difference in these two prices is
     attributable to the refinement of the designated land
     uses which came about from the Gateway Advisory
     Committee Plan study process as well as the general
     increase in residential land values over the time frame
     indicated.   The differential in price may also be
     attributable to the fact that the property may have


ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES        Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:47 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.6/12

[

     Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA          56.

          been undervalued by the seller (Daly Estate) in 1988.
          Its more recent acquisition, however, is a very good
          indicator of value for the subject property's overall
          holdings at the present time.   However, overall the
          sale price per acre should be adjusted downward now to
          account for the fact that the entire holding is larger
          and includes the Zuckerman parcel. Thus, the price per
          acre for the Daly parcel portion of the subject
          property should be adjusted downward to account for the
          fact that it is now a portion of a much larger holding.
          At the time of sale, the buyers of the subject property
          had a fairly good indication through the GAC study
          process of what would be the potential uses on the
          property.  Since the residential market has been
          essentially stable, if not declining, in some areas,
          since its acquisition, little, if any, appreciation
          could be imputed to this one separate transaction. My
          review of the sales documents also indicates that it is
          an arms-length transaction, and that some of the
          Gateway Development Group partners, while retaining a
          controlling contract with PNW, do not have a specific
          equity or ownership interest in the property.  The
          Gateway Development Group was a number of individuals,
          only several of which retained a consulting contract
          which can be terminated at any time.  They have been
          retained to assist in the approval process for the
          property and will participate in any appreciation in
          value between the acquisition/sale price indicated
          herein and the price the property would achieve based
          upon entitlements in the future (also net/excluding
          processing costs). Since I am not valuing the subject
          property, assuming entitlements are in place, this
          indicator of value as it contributes to the overall PNW
          holdings is given considerable weight since it is a
          recent indication of value agreed upon by a willing
          seller and willing buyer.   However, it has been
          compared to other land sales and it is interesting to
          note that it is in line with these other property
          transactions discussed previously.  Nothing from the
          sales documents (or conversations with the owner's
          representative) indicates that it was not an arms-
          length transaction or under circumstances that would
          require the sale to be discounted as an indicator of
          market value, particularly since it transacted when the
          residential market had already weakened substantially.



     ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES        Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:48 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.7/12




Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA        57.


     Comparable No. 10B represents the sale of the Zuckerman
portion of the subject Pacific New Wave holdings in
December of 1990. The Zuckermans sold 479.4 acres for
$9,000,000 which indicates a price per acre of $18,773.
The price per acre is substantially less than the Daly
parcel because this property has a significantly higher
acreage figure which is undevelopable due to slope and
ridge line problems.  Additionally, some portion of
the property lies outside of the City limits of Orinda
and some 80 acres is outside the Urban Limit Line.
Thus, this property sold for a lower per-acre price for
these reasons.  Terms of this transaction were
$2,000,000 cash to a $7,000,000 promissory note taken
back by the Zuckermans. The note is secured by a first
lien on the property and bears no interest.  Two
principal payments of $2,000,000 paid on July 12, 1991
and the balance is to be paid on December 12, 1991.
The property owner's representative stated the note was
current and he believed would be paid off as scheduled.
A small cash equivalent adjustment downward for the
lack of interest on the note would be indicated for
this sale in order to achieve a cash equivalent price.
Considering the constraints of this property, the sales
price should be adjusted upward in comparison to the
total assembled PNW property which has more usable
overall area, better access, and development potential
in general.  Using a 10 percent discount rate, a
reasonable cash equivalent price would be $8,400,000,
or $17,522 per acre.


     Total Acquisition Average

     For the total 978.1 acres, the total purchase price
from the two transactions which occurred fairly close
together was $25,866,708, or a price per acre of
$26,446 nominal ($25,266,708 total, or $25,832 cash
equivalent).


B.  Analysis and Conclusions -- Total 978.1 Acres

     Excluding the acquisitions of the subject property, the
market-generated comparable sales indicated a range on
a price per acre of $18,861 to a high of $65,986. The
two transactions at the high end of the range,
Comparable No. 5B and Comparable No. 9, were for
properties that were much smaller in size in comparison


ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES     Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:49 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.8/12




          Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA          58.


               to the subject property, had less development risk
          potential for achieving a reasonably equitable and
          economically feasible plan, and also were for
          properties that had significantly superior topography
          from a development standpoint. All of these would
          indicate substantial downward adjustments of these two
          higher price-per-acre sales. The lowest price-per-acre
          sale was the oldest sale, which would require typically
          the highest upward adjustment to account for the period
          of in time between its transaction and the current date
          of value. If we adjust the low-end sale upward and the
          two upper-end sales (at $60,000 per acre) downward
          substantially, there is a more consistent adjusted
          range on a price-per-acre basis of between $25,000 to
          $35,000 per acre. Not including the high and low-end
          indicators from Sale No. 1, Sale No. 5B, and Sale No.
          9, the predominant range of value indicators on a gross
          price per acre is from generally $20,000 to $34,014 as
          represented by Sale Nos. 2, 3, 4, 5A, 6, 7, and 8.
          While it is impossible to prove specific percentage
          adjustments in unique types of raw land sales
          transactions like the comparables, and the subject's
          sales, it has generally been stated throughout the
          analysis that those sales in the low $20,000 per acre
          should be essentially adjusted upward after all
          adjustments were considered. Additionally, those sales
          in excess of $30,000 per acre should be adjusted
          downward for the reasons stated herein. Thus, the
          overall analysis from the comparables would tend to
          indicate an adjusted range of between generally $25,000
          to $30,000 per acre for the subject total PNW property
          at 978.1 acres. The two separate subject land sales
          range between $18,773 to $33,821 per acre which is
          generally within (or very close to) the overall range
          before adjustments of the comparables (excluding the
          subject sales). The market sales, therefore, in my
          opinion, support the acquisition prices of the subject
          property on a broad general per-acre basis.

               The subject property is very unique due to its
          spectacular natural beauty and strategic Orinda
          location within a higher priced residential market than
          any of the comparable sales shown (with the exception
          of Comparable No. 6 which is in a custom home area of
          Walnut Creek). The general range of the properties
          between $20,000 to $34,000 per acre would have to be
          given more weight generally in this analysis and again
          were adjusted for the various major points of


          ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES     Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:50 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.9/12




Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA          59.


     difference in comparison to the subject.  The subject
has golf course, conference, limited retail, and
recreational use potential which a number of these
comparables do not have.  Conversely, most of the
comparables had superior topographical features (and
residential density potential) from a development
standpoint and the subject represents higher
development constraints because of its high slope
topography (for either residential or commercial uses.
My analysis and investigation has indicated that the
subject's golf course and resort/conference potential,
with retail element, would generally compensate for its
lower residential density potential.  This is generally
supported by the fact that its raw land value would
reflect the upside potential for the commercial land
value element of the conference, resort, and retail
potential, as well as reflecting that the golf course
has substantial upside value potential as well.  The
general comparison of Sales Nos. 2 and 4 indicated that
the Oakhurst sale, No. 2, which had golf course
potential, did not sell at a substantial discount for
the fact that it was going to have substantial open
space and a golf course as a portion of this planned
development.  Both transacted in a range of $23,500 to
$26,000 per acre on sales that transacted generally
over four years ago.  The value potential of the golf
course element is also reflected by the fact that
finished golf courses sell at fairly high values per
acre far in excess of these raw land values, although
certainly not as high as finished residential land.
For all of these reasons, the general adjusted range
for the subject lands after all factors have been
weighed is very well supported in a range of between
$25,000 to $30,000 per acre for the total property.

     In general, it must be stated that the total
acquisition price per acre for the subject property of
$26,446 would require a small downward adjustment to
account for the cash equivalent adjustments on the
Auckerman note ($25,832 was considered reasonable).  In
my opinion, however, the assemblage of these two
properties does create a slightly greater value (as an
assembled site) than the two properties considered
separately because of the ease of development and
planning of the Gateway Valley when these two
properties are combined.  Pacific New Wave recognized
that the assemblage of these two properties open up a
better planning review process for the properties and


ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES          Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:51 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.10/12


Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA                    60.


     could achieve a variety of economic and political
purposes when viewing densities, land use option
flexibility, and various community open space issues
and desires.  Although the assemblage of these two
properties does warrant a higher assemblage current
value, any increase, in my opinion, is limited by the
fact that the market is in a general slump.  During
periods when the market is in a general slump, raw land
does not typically appreciate in value significantly.
The only process that will increase the value of the
property is the granting of entitlements, since the
entitlement process has only been initiated recently,
it would be speculative and, therefore, premature to
gauge the outcome of this process.  The market would
review the historical City documents and history of the
subject property and prior specific plans considered in
the Gateway Advisory Committee process before making a
determination of its impact upon value.  However, until
these entitlement processes have been completed and the
actual approvals and a development plan approved, it is
premature to grant any value associated with the
entitlement process for the subject property at the
present time.  The acquisitions, in my opinion, are
consistent with the comparable sales outlined herein
when one gives a general consideration to the fact that
the subject has a tremendous beauty, and a unique
Orinda location (in proximity to established employment
centers, easy freeway access, proximity to the BART
system and the quaint downtown area of Orinda).  It is
also consistent given its potential for golf course,
conference, residential, and retail development uses
that are indicated under the General Plan under certain
conditions.

     My analysis overall has given consideration to all of
these factors and made adjustments to the comparables
for various major points of difference.  After these
adjustments and after discounting the high and low end
of the range, I am of the opinion that the comparables,
including (and giving weight to) the subject's specific
acquisition price on an average per-acre basis would
indicate a final adjusted value for the entire subject
lands of between $26,500 to $28,500 per acre.  A value
towards this middle portion of the adjusted range,
after discounting the high and low indicators for the
reasons given,—is warranted in this case because of the
subject's steep topography, high development costs, and
current residential, and overall economic conditions.


ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES    Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

SEP 24 '92 13:53 HENN ETZEL MELLON                                P.11/12




     Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA             61.


          No substantial increase in value, in my opinion, has
     taken place since its separate acquisitions because of
     these current economic conditions.  However, a nominal
     increase is supported by the market sales and is due to
     a small assemblage enhancement factor (which is limited
     by all of the aforementioned constraints and economic
     conditions).  The only future increase in value will
     take place through the granting of entitlements for the
     subject property and improvement in market conditions
     which could significantly enhance profitability in the
     long run in the future.

          Within the overall adjusted price-per-acre range, I am
     of the opinion that given the definition of value
     subject to the assumptions, limiting conditions and
     certification contained herein, the entire PNW property
     (at 978.1 acres) has a fair market value of $27,500 per
     acre.  This indicates a total property value for the
     entire Pacific New Wave holdings as follows:

          978.1 acres @ $27,500 Per Acre        = $26,897,750

                                      Rounded To  $26,900,000


     C.   Analysis and Conclusion -- 898.1 Acres

          In addition to requesting the total value of the 978.1
     acres, the client has requested a value of only 898.1
     acres, which excludes the southwesterly most 80-acre
     parcel designated by the County of Contra Costa as
     Assessor's Parcel No. 257-010-001.  A review of the
     City information relative to this southwesterly 80
     acres indicates that it is outside of the City of
     Orinda's City boundary line and is outside of the urban
     limit line.  Because of these factors, no development
     has ever been considered or contemplated by City
     officials or the developer on this southwesterly
     acreage.  Because of these factors, the development
     rights, or the developable acreage of the southerly
     Zuckerman property (not including the 80 acres)
     represent the main element of value of the Zuckerman
     property.  The assemblage of the Daly and Zuckerman
     properties for a potential development would not be
     substantially impacted.by the inclusion or exclusion of
     this 80 acres of land.  If the vast majority of the
     total PNW holding's value, and use potential is in the
     developable portions, and the 80 acres would not


ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES     Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

Appraisal - Pacific New Wave Property -- Orinda, CA                    62.


jeopardize the overall plan, then its incremental value
is fairly low.  Given its location outside of the City
limits, and lack of proposed use potential, as well as
considering that it will remain open space, these
factors all combine to support its low incremental
value.  Given all of these factors, the balance of the
property at 898.1 acre constitutes the vast majority of
the overall value of the Pacific New Wave holdings
valued previously at $26,900,000.  This is because the
898.1 acres has all of the developable acreage and
theoretical development rights.  If one was to simply
divide the total property value by the 898.1 acres, the
per-acre value indicated would be $29,952.

As stated previously, the comparables, when viewing the
entire 978.1-acre property, indicated a final adjusted
range of between $26,500 to $28,500 per acre.
Considering that the potential for development and use
is substantially within the 898.1 acres, a slight
adjustment upward of this range would be indicated when
valuing just the 898.1 acres.  My appraised value of
the total holdings was $27,500 per acre.  In my
opinion, when valuing only the 898.1 acres, which
represents the vast majority, if not all of the value
of the PNW holdings, I am of the opinion that a
slightly higher value per acre of $29,500 is more
appropriate.  This is still well within the general
adjusted range of $25,000 to $30,000 per acre indicated
by the comparable sales, and considers that the
incremental value (and use) of the 80 acres is limited.
This would appear to impute a slight value to the
additional acreage; however, I must state that I am not
valuing the 80 acres separately but simply valuing the
898.1 acres directly after first viewing the overall
value.  Given all of these factors, I am of the opinion
that the 898.1 acres, which excludes the southerly 80-
acre parcel, would be fairly valued at $29,500 per acre
as follows:

898.1 Acres X $29,500 Per Acre              = $26,493,950

                                  Rounded To $26,500,000
                                            ___________




ROLAND H. BURCHARD & ASSOCIATES     Real Estate Appraisers & Consultants

sections.question: Can a member of the Orinda Planning Commission participate in governmental decisions regarding real property in which a first deed of trust is held by a bank for which the Planning Commissioner is an employee?
sections.facts:
Mr. James Roethe is a member of the Orinda Planning Commission. He is employed by the Bank of America as the director of litigation. As an officer of the bank, he receives income in the form of a salary and is also entitled to receive options to acquire stock in the Bank of America at predetermined prices. The Bank of America is a Fortune 500 corporation.

The Orinda Planning Commission is processing an application for the development of a project in the Gateway Valley area of the City of Orinda. The project is a proposal by Pacific New Wave Corporation and is generally described as the "Gateway Valley 90/10 Plan". The project consists of approximately 978 acres and proposes a conference center, golf course facilities, multiple family units, single family lots and permanently protected open space. The project area consists of two parcels, one of 499 acres and one of 479 acres which total 978 acres. One of the parcels comprising the project area of 978 acres is known as the Daly property and consists of 499 acres.

Scott Hallabrin  
September 30, 1992  

The former Security Pacific Bank loaned $6.5 million to Pacific New Wave Corporation secured by a first deed of trust on the 499 acre parcel (the Daly property). The Security Pacific Bank recently merged with the Bank of America so that this loan is now held in the loan portfolio of the Bank of America.

To date, the processing of the Gateway Valley 90/10 Plan has involved a series of public hearings before the Planning Commission on the EIR and public hearings on various aspects of the Plan itself. Mr. Roethe participated in these hearings because he was not then employed with the Bank of America.

Future proceedings in which the Planning Commission will be involved with the decision-making process on the project will include a request to amend the Orinda General Plan in order to conform with the project, the preparation and recommendation for adoption of a specific plan for the Gateway Valley, the rezoning of property in the Gateway Valley to permit development of a project as finally approved, the subdivision of the property into separate parcels and the processing of a development agreement. The Planning Commission will be a participant in each of these steps.

GENERAL COMMENTS

I have had the benefit of reviewing your responses to the following requests for advice: your file numbers I-88-147, I-89-466, I-89-537 and I-91-071. These involve in one way or another whether a public official can participate in a governmental decision regarding real property on which a bank holds a security in property or has some indirect connection with a developer and there is some connection between the public official and the bank.

Since the Bank could be affected indirectly by a decision of the Orinda Planning Commission, it appears that the appropriate standard for determining whether or not a decision will materially affect the Bank is determined under regulation 18702.2.

The following information is submitted on the issue of whether there would be any foreseeable financial effect on the Bank of America irrespective of whether or not the Commission acts favorably or unfavorably on the project, having in mind the threshold for disqualification.1/

First, I enclose portions of an appraisal of the entire 978 acre parcel dated November 1991 prepared by Roland H. Burchard. The appraisal puts a fair market value as of November 1991 of $26,900,000 on the entire 978 acre site. However, the $6.5 million loan which the Bank holds is secured by the

1/I understand the threshold amounts under Regulation 18702.2(a) to be: will the Commission’s decision (a) increase or decrease the Bank’s gross revenues by $1,000,000 or more or (b) result in the Bank incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses of $250,000 or more.

Scott Hallabrin  
September 30, 1992  

Daly property only consisting of 499 acres. I call your attention to pp. 34 and 55-56 of the appraisal for a discussion of the Daly property. The sale of this parcel to Pacific New Wave in September 1990 was for a price of $16.8 million. Extrapolating values from the appraisal for the Bank secured parcel it appears that the present value of that parcel exceeds the amount of the loan by an amount sufficient to insure that any action by the Commission would not affect the value of the property in a manner which would materially affect the Bank.

Second, I have discussed with representatives of the Bank of America whether a decision affecting the applicant would affect the Bank assuming the existence of certain facts. For example, one assumption is that the value of the property securing the loan equals or exceeds the amount of the loan, and whether or not the Commission acts on an entitlement, the Bank’s ability to achieve repayment of the loan would not be affected. The other assumption is if there were a default of the loan and the Bank foreclosed, the expenses incurred by the Bank would not exceed $250,000. Civil Code section 2924d(b) imposes a cap on foreclosure costs of 1% of the loan amount ($65,000). While it is possible that litigation attendant to foreclosure and/or bankruptcy of the debtor could ensue, thus increasing the costs to the Bank, these are events which are not reasonably foreseeable. Representatives of the Bank have indicated that if the facts were to be as stated (without reference to costs over and above normal foreclosure costs), the financial effect on the Bank would not in their opinion reach the threshold amounts.

Finally, we do not see that there is a disqualifying nexus as described in regulation section 18702.1(d) because Mr. Roethe does not receive, nor will he receive, income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided or hindered by a decision of the Planning Commission.

Thank you for your consideration.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Can a member of the Orinda Planning Commission participate in governmental decisions regarding real property in which a first deed of trust is held by a bank for which the Planning Commissioner is an employee?
embedding.first_500_words: September 30, 1992 Scott Hallabrin, Esq. Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission Post Office Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Re: Request for Written Advice (Government Code §83114(b)) Dear Mr. Hallabrin: This letter requests the Commission to provide written advice with respect to the duties of a member of the Orinda Planning Commission under the Conflict of Interest Provisions of the Political Reform Act. QUESTION Can a member of the Orinda Planning Commission participate in governmental decisions regarding real property in which a first deed of trust is held by a bank for which the Planning Commissioner is an employee? FACTS Mr. James Roethe is a member of the Orinda Planning Commission. He is employed by the Bank of America as the director of litigation. As an officer of the bank, he receives income in the form of a salary and is also entitled to receive options to acquire stock in the Bank of America at predetermined prices. The project area consists of two parcels, one of 499 acres and one of 479 acres which total 978 acres. One of the parcels comprising the project area of 978 acres is known as the Daly property and consists of 499 acres. The Security Pacific Bank recently merged with the Bank of America so that this loan is now held in the loan portfolio of the Bank of America. To date, the processing of the Gateway Valley 90/10 Plan has involved a series of public hearings before the Planning Commission on the EIR and public hearings on various aspects of the Plan itself. Mr. Roethe participated in these hearings because he was not then employed with the Bank of America.
